architecture.summary: SQL-PaLM is a comprehensive framework for Text-to-SQL that adapts PaLM-2 (Unicorn variant) using both few-shot prompting and instruction fine-tuning. For prompting, it applies execution-based consistency decoding with error filtering: multiple SQL outputs are sampled at high temperature, executed against the database, and aggregated by execution result to select the most probable valid output. For fine-tuning, the model is trained end-to-end on serialized database schema, auxiliary content (descriptions, values, hints), and natural language questions to generate SQL. The framework incorporates column selection (program-aided or retrieval-based) to handle large-scale databases by reducing input length, and test-time refinement that integrates outputs from multiple training paradigms using execution feedback.

biblio.title: SQL-PaLM: Improved Large Language Model Adaptation for Text-to-SQL (extended)

claims.statement: Mixing training data with diverse sets, despite having various formats, can benefit LLMs, indicating the potential of tuned LLMs for superior generalization ability. Complex SQL data particularly have been observed to be more beneficial as tuning data.
Relevant subset of database content (descriptions, values) included in the prompt improves performance while irrelevant information can harm the performance.
Hard column selection significantly reduces the length of the inputs going into the LLMs while yielding negligible impact to performance.
Program-aided column selection has higher column selection accuracy, whereas retrieval-based approach is more cost-effective and controllable.
Soft column selection, which does not exclude unselected columns, has higher performance than hard column selection, albeit it necessitates LLMs with longer context length.
Test-time execution-based selection approach integrates multiple setups (prompting vs. tuning paradigms) to further improve performance.
The holistic SQL-PaLM approach yields substantial advancements in Text-to-SQL, as demonstrated on two key public benchmarks, Spider and BIRD.
Tuning large LLMs for Text-to-SQL improves performance in challenging scenarios with complex SQL, semantic constraints, and large-scale databases compared to few-shot prompting alone.
